text: The video shows a large, complex incisional hernia with minimal adhesions, primarily located in the upper portion of the hernia defect. Most of the adhesions are omental.

The video shows a large, complex incisional hernia with minimal adhesions, primarily located in the upper portion of the hernia defect. The adhesions are mostly omental, and the adhesiolysis process is straightforward due to the absence of intestinal involvement.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis, revealing the liver densely adherent to the anterior abdominal wall in the subxiphoid region. The dissection progresses to develop the retromuscular space and incise the posterior rectus sheath, ensuring adequate cephaloid overlap for mesh placement.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis, carefully dissecting the liver from its dense adhesion to the anterior abdominal wall near the subxiphoid region. The omentum is being separated, and the retromuscular space is developed by incising the posterior rectus sheath to ensure adequate cephaloid overlap for mesh placement.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis, carefully dissecting the omentum and part of the liver from the anterior abdominal wall. During the dissection, the surgeon encounters an unexpected structure, which is confirmed not to be the colon or stomach, as these organs are not in proximity. The surgeon examines the pylorus and the first portion of the duodenum, ruling out bowel involvement.

The surgeon encounters an unexpected structure during dissection, initially uncertain of its identity. The colon and stomach are visually confirmed to be distant from the area. The surgeon inspects the pylorus and first portion of the duodenum, ruling out bowel involvement. A Raytech is used to collect contents from the structure, avoiding contamination and spillage.
The surgeon continues the dissection, carefully placing a Raytech sponge to collect any contents and prevent contamination, ensuring no spillage occurs during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully expresses a substance resembling anchovy paste, containing it within an EndoCatch bag and a Raytech to prevent spillage during the dissection.

The surgeon continues to express a substance resembling anchovy paste from a cavity, carefully containing it within an EndoCatch bag and a Raytech to prevent spillage. The cavity is cleaned out, revealing what is suspected to be a retained gallstone that has granulated over time.
The surgeon cleans a small crater, suspected to be a retained gallstone that has become granulated over time following a previous cholecystectomy.
The surgeon retrieves the Raytech containing the collected contents and cauterizes the liver bed area to eliminate any residual material.

The surgeon removes the Raytech containing the contents from the surgical site and cauterizes the liver bed to eliminate any residual material. The EndoCatch bag is extracted, and clean instruments are introduced. A small portion of the liver is dissected from the anterior abdominal wall, allowing for the incision of the posterior rectus sheath in the upper abdomen. The liver separates easily with the dissection.

The surgeon removes the EndoCatch bag and replaces the instruments with clean ones. A small portion of the liver is dissected from the anterior abdominal wall. The surgeon aims to incise the posterior rectus sheath in the upper abdomen to perform upper dissection, targeting preperitoneal and subside voidal areas.
The surgeon performs dissection to separate the liver from the surrounding tissue. Once sufficient separation is achieved, the surgeon begins making an incision in the posterior rectus sheath.

The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath just off the midline, initiating the retromuscular dissection and development of the retromuscular space. The incision is made near the liver edge, but the sheath is successfully incised.

The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath just off midline, initiating the retromuscular dissection and development of the retromuscular space. The incision is made near the liver edge, but the posterior rectus sheath is successfully incised.

The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath along the planned dissection length in the upper abdomen, developing the retromuscular plane laterally towards the linea semilunaris while carefully avoiding injury to visible neurovascular bundles.

The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath along the planned dissection length in the upper abdomen, carefully developing the retromuscular plane laterally towards the linea semilunaris. The dissection is performed with caution to avoid injuring a large neurovascular bundle visible in the area, while navigating around the liver.

The surgeon navigates over the liver to continue incising the posterior rectus sheath, extending the dissection laterally. The plan involves connecting these dissection points upon converting to the contralateral side.

The surgeon carefully navigates around a large neurovascular bundle in the upper abdomen, preserving it while continuing the dissection. The liver is left undisturbed as the surgeon progresses downward.

The surgeon continues the retromuscular dissection in the upper abdomen, ensuring the posterior rectus sheath is incised below the arcuate line. The dissection progresses towards the suprapubic area, approaching the preperitoneal plane near the space of Retzius, while noting the absence of any inferior hernia components.

The surgeon ensures the retromuscular dissection extends as planned, focusing on the crossover in the suprapubic area. The posterior rectus sheath is dissected down below the arcuate line, transitioning into the preperitoneal plane towards the space of Retzius.
The surgeon incises the transversalis fascia, revealing the pubic bone below, while dissecting low in the abdominal region without entering the space of Retzius.

The surgeon incises the transversalis fascia to access the pre-peritoneal plane, creating a lateral tunnel while preserving the round ligament. The pubic bone is visible below, and the transversus abdominis muscle fibers are identified and separated from the posterior elements.
The surgeon attempts to enter the true pre-peritoneal plane, carefully digging a tunnel laterally. The round ligament is visible but remains undivided, as the patient does not have inguinal hernias.

The surgeon creates a lateral tunnel, identifying the transversus abdominis muscle fibers. The muscle fibers are separated from the posterior elements, marking the beginning of the bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR).

The surgeon begins the bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR) by separating the transversus abdominis muscle fibers from the posterior components, moving from right to left. The transversalis fascia is left intact in the upper abdomen to provide additional strength to the posterior sheath. The dissection progresses laterally, creating separation between the transversus abdominis and the posterior elements.

The surgeon separates the transversus abdominis muscle fibers from the posterior components, moving from right to left in the surgical field. The dissection progresses until reaching the upper abdomen, where the surgeon transitions to a top-down TAR approach.

The surgeon opens the preperitoneal plane by incising the posterior lamella of the internal oblique and the transversus abdominis muscle fibers, while leaving the transversalis fascia intact in the upper abdomen. The thin peritoneum is visible posterior to the muscle fibers.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the transversus abdominis muscle, separating it from the posterior elements. The dissection proceeds laterally and top-down, aiming to connect the upper and lower TAR dissections.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the transversus abdominis muscle from top down, carefully staying medial to the neurovascular bundles to avoid disrupting the semilunar line. The bulky transversus abdominis muscle transitions from muscular to aponeurotic as the dissection meets the inferior area, separating the muscle from the transversalis fascia. The transition between the preperitoneal and pre-transversalis spaces becomes visible.

The surgeon carefully dissects medial to the neurovascular bundles to avoid disrupting the semilunar line, preventing lateral hernia defects. The bulky transversus abdominis muscle is visible, transitioning from muscular to aponeurotic tissue as the dissection meets the inferior area.
The surgeon separates the transversus abdominis (TA) muscle from the underlying transversalis fascia, revealing the transition between the preperitoneal and pre-transversalis spaces.

The surgeon transitions between the preperitoneal and pre-transversalis spaces, revealing the transversalis fascia below in the upper abdomen and on the transversus abdominis muscle towards the lower abdomen. The lateral dissection extends posteriorly towards the psoas muscle.

The surgeon continues dissection on the transversus abdominis muscle towards the lower abdomen, extending the lateral dissection posteriorly towards the psoas muscle. The posterior rectus sheath is positioned atop the viscera without tension. In the upper abdomen, the diaphragm fibers are visible as they meet the transversus abdominis fibers. The liver, previously adherent to the posterior sheath, is gently brought down during the dissection, allowing for cephalad overlap of the mesh.

The surgeon continues dissection in the upper abdomen, revealing the diaphragm fibers meeting the transversus abdominis fibers. The posterior rectus sheath is positioned atop the viscera without tension. The liver, initially adherent to the posterior sheath, is gently separated and lowered as the dissection progresses to the contralateral side.

The surgeon continues opening in the cephalad direction to ensure adequate overlap of the mesh. The dissection extends into the space of Retzius, although not fully completed due to the absence of hernia defects. A small hole in the peritoneum is closed using a 3-0 Vicryl suture in a figure-of-eight pattern. Contralateral ports are placed as the right-sided TAR is completed.

The surgeon performs dissection into the space of Retzius, placing mesh into the pelvis. A small hole in the peritoneum is closed using a 3-0 Vicryl suture in a figure-of-eight fashion. Contralateral ports are placed, and the right-sided TAR is completed. The boom is flipped and redocked for the left-sided dissection. The surgeon stays in the preperitoneal space, incising peritoneum for potential soft tissue coverage. The posterior sheath is incised, and dissection is carried into the subxiphoid space above the liver, ensuring at least five centimeters of mesh overlap above the defect.

The surgeon flips the boom and redocks to perform the left-sided dissection, staying in the preperitoneal space. An incision is made in the peritoneum near the subxiphoid area to preserve tissue for potential visceral coverage. The posterior sheath is incised on the left side.

The surgeon continues the dissection into the subxiphoid space within the preperitoneal plane above the liver, extending at least five centimeters for mesh overlap. The dissection progresses along the posterior rectus sheath down into the lower abdomen.

The surgeon continues dissection along the posterior rectus sheath into the lower abdomen, entering the preperitoneal space below the arcuate line. The dissection meets the right-sided and suprapubic crossover dissections. Transversus abdominis muscle fibers are visible in the upper abdomen, while retromuscular dissection extends laterally to the semilunar line, preserving neurovascular bundles. Port sites are visible, which will be closed with sutures in a figure-of-eight fashion after completing the TAR procedure.

The surgeon enters the preperitoneal space below the arcuate line, connecting with the right-sided dissection and suprapubic crossover dissection. The retromuscular dissection is completed along the length before deciding on further lateral dissection or a bottoms-up TAR approach.

The surgeon identifies transversus abdominis fibers in the upper abdomen and continues the retromuscular dissection laterally towards the semilunar line, carefully preserving visible neurovascular bundles.

The surgeon dissects around the port sites in the lower abdomen, closing the three port holes with a 3-0 Vicryl suture in a figure-of-eight fashion. In the preperitoneal space, the round ligament is visible on the patient's left side as the posterior elements are separated from the lateral abdominal wall.

In the preperitoneal space, the surgeon identifies the round ligament on the patient's left side and separates the posterior elements from the lateral abdominal wall. The transversus abdominis muscle fibers are visible laterally, and a tunnel is created. The surgeon opts for a top-down TAR approach, ensuring diaphragm fibers remain above to prevent a Morgagni hernia. The posterior lamella of the internal oblique is opened, and the transversus abdominis muscle fibers are divided, allowing entry into the plane to separate components from the posterior elements in the lateral abdominal wall.

The surgeon visualizes the transversus abdominis muscle fibers laterally and begins creating a tunnel. The diaphragm fibers are visible, and care is taken to ensure they remain positioned above to prevent a iatrogenic Morgagni hernia.

The surgeon opens the posterior lamella of the internal oblique and divides the transversus abdominis muscle fibers, entering the plane to separate components from the posterior elements in the lateral abdominal wall. The dissection extends laterally, ensuring the diaphragm muscle fibers remain elevated. The posterior sheath is removed from the exposed transversus abdominis muscle.

The surgeon continues the lateral dissection, removing the posterior sheath from the raw transversus abdominis muscle. The diaphragm muscle fibers are kept elevated. As the dissection progresses inferiorly, the transition from the pre-transversalis to the pre-peritoneal space is visible. The aponeurotic portion of the transversus abdominis is opened near a port site, completing the dissection and connecting the upper and lower dissection areas.

The surgeon transitions from the pre-transversalis to the pre-peritoneal space on the patient's left side, opening the aponeurotic portion of the transversus abdominis near a port site, completing the dissection and connecting the upper and lower dissections.

The surgeon re-approximates the posterior rectus sheath using a two-oh V-lock suture, ensuring the layer comes together without tension. The omentum, falciform, and liver provide additional tissue for buttressing if needed.

The surgeon uses a two-oh V-lock suture to close the posterior layer without tension, utilizing parts of the falciform ligament to re-approximate both sides due to the weight of the liver. The port site holes in the peritoneum are closed with a three ovicle suture in a figure of eight fashion. For larger defects, a number one non-absorbable V-lock suture is used, taking bites through the linea alba and hernia sac to obliterate dead space, with the needle placed in the fascia for ease of tightening.

The surgeon uses parts of the falciform ligament to re-approximate both sides of the posterior layer, ensuring there are no holes. Various materials like omentum or vical mesh can be used to buttress any openings, but typically, the existing tissue provides adequate coverage for closure.

The surgeon begins suturing from below, meeting the sutures in the middle, and ties them together. The port site holes in the peritoneum are closed using a 3-0 Vicryl suture in a figure-of-eight pattern.
The surgeon closes a large defect using a number one non-absorbable V-lock suture, performing multiple throws and sequentially tightening the suture to manage its length and ensure secure closure.

The surgeon takes parts of the linea alba and the hernia sac to obliterate the dead space. The needle is inserted into the fascia to secure the suture during the tightening process, ensuring it remains accessible throughout the procedure.

The surgeon takes multiple bites of the anterior rectus sheath edges on each side of the rectus muscle, preparing to approximate the tissue from below without tightening the suture until the approximation from above and below is complete.

The surgeon begins re-approximating the anterior rectus sheath using number one V-lock sutures, with pneumoperitoneum reduced to eight millimeters of mercury. The large football-shaped defect is gradually transformed into a smaller ellipse-shaped defect. Care is taken to avoid buttonholing or dimpling the skin while taking bites of the hernia sac, ensuring good fascia bites without involving much muscle. The suture is tightened sequentially to obliterate the space and minimize seroma formation, with a drain left in place.

The surgeon uses multiple number one V-lock sutures to re-approximate the anterior rectus sheath, with the pneumoperitoneum decreased to eight millimeters of mercury for better visualization and manipulation.

The surgeon carefully takes shallow bites of the hernia sac, ensuring the thin skin above is not tented or dimpled, while aiming to obliterate the space to minimize seroma formation. A drain is left in place to assist with fluid management.
The surgeon carefully takes bites of the fascia, avoiding the muscle, and performs multiple throws of the suture before tightening it.
The surgeon sequentially tightens the suture down on the anterior rectus sheath, ensuring the needle remains visible for easy retrieval.

The surgeon secures the needle in the fascia to prevent loss during suturing along the incision length. Using a V-lock suture, they reinforce areas of weak fascia and prepare to accommodate a 30x30 centimeter mesh piece.
The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to sew along the entire length of the incision, reinforcing areas with potentially weak fascia. The suture is sewn back on itself to ensure secure closure.

The surgeon measures the space and places a 30 by 30 centimeter bioabsorbable Phasix mesh within the retromuscular space. The mesh is laid flat, and a fibrin sealant is sprayed over it. A drain is then placed to complete the procedure.
The surgeon places a bioabsorbable Phasix mesh within the retromuscular space, ensuring it lays flat. A fibrin sealant is sprayed over the mesh, and a drain is positioned to complete the procedure.
